text: Offloading data traffic from cellular to Wi-Fi serves as a prime example of inter-radio access technology (RAT) coordination. While this strategy clearly mitigates congestion on overloaded cellular networks, the fundamental limits of such offloading and its broader impact on system-wide performance remain poorly understood. To address this gap, we develop a comprehensive and tractable analytical framework comprising $M$ distinct RATs. Each technology features up to $K$ tiers of access points (APs) characterized by unique transmit powers, path loss exponents, deployment densities, and bandwidth allocations. We model each AP class as an independent Poisson point process (PPP) and represent mobile user locations through a separate, independent PPP, with all channels subject to i.i.d. Rayleigh fading. Using this framework, we derive the rate distribution across the entire network under a weighted association strategy, where weights can be adjusted to optimize specific performance metrics. Our analysis reveals that the optimal offloading ratio for maximizing SINR coverage generally differs from the ratio required to maximize rate coverage, defined as the proportion of users achieving a minimum throughput threshold.
